turns.text: Hello Carey, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s nice to meet you. Honestly, thank you for asking. It’s strange—I spent so much of my career being the one asking the questions, navigating the clinical side of reproductive health for other people, and now I’m the one sitting on the edge of the exam table, feeling very much like a patient.

At forty, with this being my second, I thought I’d be an old pro. Instead, I find myself plagued by the "medium risk" label and the fact that I know exactly what every acronym on my chart means. It’s a double-edged sword. My OB is wonderful—she treats me like a peer, which I love, but there are days I wish I could just turn off the medical part of my brain. I’ll be sitting there, watching the ultrasound, and instead of just feeling that rush of joy, I’m mentally assessing every measurement and calculating the "what-ifs."

I’m thirty-four weeks now, and the house in South Hadley is finally starting to feel like a nursery. But then a shift happens—I feel a kick, or a wave of exhaustion hits me—and the anxiety settles in. I’m just trying to balance the clinical data in my head with the hope that my body is ready for this. It’s… it’s a lot to hold all at once.
Tell me about the midwives, doctors, and other healthcare professionals who were part of your pregnancy care. Which relationships felt most important to you, and why?

My OB is the cornerstone of it all, really. Because of my background, I’ve worked with plenty of providers who talk *at* patients, or who gloss over the details to keep things simple. She doesn’t do that. When I’m in her office, we talk almost like colleagues, which is exactly what I thought I wanted. She respects my clinical literacy, which makes me feel seen and validated.

But if I’m being completely honest? Sometimes that transparency feels like a heavy weight. There’s a part of me that desperately wants to be "managed," to be told "everything will be fine" without the caveat of a statistical probability. When she explains the nuances of my chart, I find myself spiraling into the "what-ifs" of my age.

I haven't had much interaction with the wider team yet, but the nurses—they’re the ones who really ground me. When I’m feeling that irrational, late-night anxiety, it’s not the clinical breakdown I need; it’s the human touch, the simple reassurance that my vitals are textbook-perfect, and the reminder to just breathe. That’s the relationship that feels the most critical right now. I’m starting to realize that as much as I value the clinical expertise, I’m craving someone to remind me that I’m a mother, not just a case study.
Who else was involved in your care that you'd like to mention — community midwives, consultants, support workers, interpreters?

To be honest, it’s mostly been me, my husband, and my OB. We haven’t really branched out into the community side of things—no doulas or specialized consultants—partly because I’ve spent so long navigating these systems that I felt like I could handle the "management" myself. It’s that professional habit, I suppose. I keep waiting for the point where I feel like I need more, but I’m hesitant to bring new voices into the mix when I’m already feeling a bit overwhelmed by the clinical noise I have in my head.

My husband is my primary support, obviously. He’s the one who sees the look on my face when I’ve been staring at the monitor for too long, or when I’m deep in a PubMed rabbit hole at 2:00 a.m. He’s the one who gently tells me to close the laptop and just sit with the baby for a while. 

There’s a comfort in keeping the circle small. I worry that if I invite too many people in, the conversation will shift even further toward the "what-ifs" of my age rather than the quiet, simple experience I’m trying to create for myself. I’m just trying to stay in the present, tucked away here in South Hadley, before the reality of the hospital sets in.
Tell me about your partner's — or your closest support person's — involvement during your pregnancy appointments and day-to-day experience. How did their presence or absence shape things for you?

My husband is my anchor, though I’m sure he’d tell you it’s not an easy job. Because I know the language of the clinic, I tend to hold onto things—I’ll come home from an appointment and play it cool, but he knows the tension in my shoulders. He doesn't need to be a medical expert; he just needs to be the person who reminds me that I am more than my age or my "medium risk" chart.

In those moments when the clinical, analytical part of my brain starts taking over, he’s the one who gently pulls me back. He’ll make tea, or we’ll walk through the nursery and just talk about names or the changing leaves outside our window. His presence keeps me from drifting off into the data. 

When he is at the appointments, he acts as a buffer. He asks the questions I’m sometimes too "smart" to ask—the simple, human questions. Having him there means I don’t have to hold all the emotional space by myself. If I’m honest, I think his calm is the only thing that’s kept me from panicking during the third trimester. I’m the expert, but he’s the partner, and right now, I think I need that partnership a lot more than I need any extra medical consultation.
How did that affect your relationship with each other — did pregnancy bring you closer together or create tensions?

It’s been both, honestly. It’s impossible for a pregnancy to be anything but a massive shift, especially at forty. We’ve been married a while, and we had a very established rhythm before this. Adding a baby into the mix—especially with all the extra mental baggage I’m carrying—has definitely tested that rhythm.

There are moments of incredible closeness, like when we’re in the nursery or just lying in bed talking about who the baby will be. Those are the times I feel like we’re a team again. But then, there’s the tension of my own making. My husband sees me spiraling, and he tries to offer comfort, but sometimes his optimism feels a little... naive to me. I feel a flash of irritation, thinking, *“You don’t know the risks like I do,”* and then I immediately feel guilty for shutting him out. I’m carrying this heavy clinical weight, and sometimes I accidentally put that burden on him, too. 

He’s incredibly patient, but I know it’s hard for him to watch me be so anxious when I’ve spent my life helping others stay calm. I think we’re learning to navigate a new version of "us"—one that’s a bit more vulnerable. It’s not always seamless, but we’re finding our way. I just hope he knows how much I need him to be the one who doesn't look at the charts.

Tell me about the people — friends, family, online communities — who you turned to most during your pregnancy when you had questions or worries. How did those relationships work alongside your formal care?

That’s a complicated one. To be honest, I’ve been somewhat guarded about who I turn to. I haven't joined any online pregnancy forums or "mom groups," and I think that’s intentional. Because of my work background, I’ve seen the way misinformation spreads in those spaces, and I think that would only trigger my anxiety further. I don’t need someone else’s worst-case scenario added to my own internal library of them.

My circle is very small. I have a couple of friends who are also in the field, and we have a tacit agreement: we keep the talk professional and supportive, but we don’t commiserate over the "horror stories." That boundary is vital. If I’m feeling really overwhelmed, I might reach out to a trusted colleague just to clarify a clinical doubt, but I’m very careful not to let that replace my OB’s care. 

Ultimately, I find that my friends and family—the ones outside of medicine—are my best escape. They don't know the acronyms. When I talk to them, I’m just Carey, the woman having a baby, not the clinician with a "medium risk" chart. They provide a necessary, normal world that exists entirely outside of the medical gaze. It’s the only place where I can genuinely turn off that analytical voice in my head.
Who else was involved that perhaps you didn't expect to lean on — an online group, a work colleague, a community figure?

Actually, I’ve found myself leaning on my physical therapist, of all people. I didn't expect to share much beyond my back pain and the pelvic strain that comes with being thirty-four weeks, but she’s become an unexpected touchstone. 

Because she works in a clinical setting but isn't part of my obstetric care team, there’s this specific, neutral distance there. She sees the physical toll, but she doesn't treat me like a high-risk medical case. She just treats my body. When I’m on her table, the conversation isn't about my age or my "medium risk" status; it’s about alignment, breath, and the mechanics of movement. 

It’s been incredibly grounding to have someone focus on my *physical* reality in a way that feels empowering rather than diagnostic. She doesn’t know the jargon in my chart, and she doesn't ask about it. She just asks how I’m breathing and where I’m holding my tension. It’s a strange relief to have a professional in my life who isn't interested in my pathology. It’s just me, her, and the task of helping my body finish this marathon. I never thought I’d value that kind of non-obstetric clinical support so much, but it’s become one of the few hours a week where I actually feel like I’m in control of my own health.

Tell me about a time during your pregnancy when you had to make a significant decision about a test, a procedure, or a care option. Who was involved in that decision with you, and how did each person's input land?

Early in the second trimester, we had to decide on the extent of the genetic screening. That was a rough patch. In my world, it’s all about gathering as much data as possible—it’s how we operate, right? You analyze, you prepare, you mitigate. I had all the literature pulled up, the statistics memorized, and I was ready to dive into the most comprehensive testing available.

My husband, though, he pushed back. He was the one who asked the hardest question: "What will this data change for us?" He wasn't saying no to the testing, but he was forcing me to confront the *intent* behind it. He saw that I wasn't doing it just for clinical necessity; I was doing it because I was terrified that if I didn't know every single detail, I wouldn't be "prepared" enough to be a good mother.

It was a difficult conversation. I felt a surge of professional frustration—I wanted to rely on my expertise—but he was right. We sat in our kitchen for hours, arguing more about my anxiety than the medical procedure itself. Ultimately, we went with a slightly more moderate testing path. When I look back, I realize that his input saved me from drowning in even more data I didn't actually need. He helped me realize that being a mother isn't about having a perfect, risk-free chart; it’s about choosing to move forward even when you don't have all the answers. That decision wasn't just clinical—it was the moment I started to let go of control.

Tell me about how your relationships with people outside the healthcare system — your employer, your community, your cultural or faith community — shaped your pregnancy experience. Who was supportive, who added pressure?

My professional life has been both a sanctuary and a source of friction. Being in this field, there’s an unspoken expectation that I should be the "model patient"—that I should handle pregnancy with this graceful, clinical efficiency. My colleagues are supportive, but their support often feels like, "Oh, you know the drill, you're fine." It’s isolating because I’m expected to have all the answers, when in reality, I’m feeling just as fragile as anyone else. I’ve had to set firm boundaries to stop people from asking me for clinical opinions when I’m clearly struggling with my own pregnancy. That’s been hard; saying "I’m just the patient right now" feels like pulling rank, but I have to do it to survive.

Outside of work, the community in South Hadley has been warm, but there’s a quiet, persistent pressure from the "ideal" version of motherhood. People see the gray in my hair or hear that I’m forty and offer these "encouraging" platitudes about being a "geriatric" mother that just grate on my nerves. It’s a strange, subtle form of judgment. 

I’ve found myself retreating from some of those social circles. I don’t have the energy to perform or to justify my choices to people who don't understand the nuance of my medical background. I’m finding more peace in the quiet—just me, my husband, and the baby—than in the outside world.
How did that affect your sense of identity — were there moments when being pregnant felt in tension with other parts of who you are?

Every single day. It’s a constant tug-of-war between the clinician and the mother, and I’m losing the ability to tell them apart. Professionally, I spent twenty years training myself to be objective—to see a pregnancy as a series of markers, a sequence of risks to be mitigated. Now, I am that sequence. I look at my own belly and I see the fetal growth percentiles, the placenta positioning, the potential for complications that my age makes statistically more likely. It’s like trying to watch a sunset while simultaneously calculating the exact refraction of light through the atmosphere. It ruins the view.

There’s a real tension there. Part of my identity is built on being the "expert," the one who knows what’s going on and stays calm under pressure. But this baby? This is the most uncontrollable, unpredictable thing I’ve ever been part of. There’s no clinical protocol for the feeling of being forty and realizing your body is doing something that—logically—it shouldn't be doing this smoothly. 

I’ve had to mourn the version of myself who thought she could "think" her way through a pregnancy. I’m realizing that I can’t research my way into a perfect outcome. It’s deeply uncomfortable to surrender that authority, but I’m learning that the "expert" identity doesn't actually offer any protection from the fear. It’s just an illusion of control.

Tell me about the moments in your pregnancy when your body was being examined or discussed by healthcare professionals. Who was present during those interactions, and how did their presence affect how you felt?

Those moments are when the "clinical mask" I wear at work starts to slip, and it’s always when I’m most vulnerable. My husband is almost always there. When it’s just the two of us with my OB, there’s a protective bubble. I appreciate that she’s careful—she’ll pause and explain things in a way that acknowledges my expertise without stripping me of the patient experience. 

But there have been times, particularly during those "medium risk" monitoring sessions, where a technician or a covering resident has come in. That’s when it gets complicated. They see my chart, they see the age, they see the professional credentials listed in my history, and their tone changes. They start using shortcuts or, conversely, they start over-explaining things in a way that feels a bit patronizing, as if they’re nervous about having a "peer" in the room. 

I’ve found myself becoming very defensive in those moments. I’ll catch myself correcting their terminology or asking for the specific data points behind a comment they made. My husband hates when I do that; he can see me retreating back into the "clinician" role as a defense mechanism to keep the fear at bay. When he reaches over and takes my hand, it’s a physical tether back to reality. It reminds me that I’m not there to lecture or critique—I’m there to have a baby. I’m just trying to survive those moments without feeling like a specimen.
Were there times when having — or not having — someone with you in those clinical moments made a real difference to how safe or exposed you felt?

The difference is night and day. There’s a profound level of exposure when you’re stripped of your professional armor and sitting in a gown, especially when the staff sees your credentials and immediately starts testing your knowledge or over-explaining. 

When my husband is there, the room feels like a private space. He’s my boundary. If a resident starts speaking to me like a colleague rather than a patient, or if I start "lecturing" to mask my anxiety, he’s the one who interjects with something grounding—like a question about how the baby is doing or a joke to lighten the clinical atmosphere. Without him, I feel nakedly exposed. I feel the urge to "perform" competence because I’m so terrified that if I don’t sound like an expert, I won’t be treated with the same level of care or respect.

On the one or two occasions where I went to a routine check-in alone, I felt incredibly vulnerable. I found myself hyper-vigilant, parsing every word the medical staff used, trying to deduce if they were holding something back from me. It’s an exhausting way to exist. Having my husband there doesn’t just make me feel safer; it allows me the grace to stop being the "expert" and just be a woman who is growing a life. It’s the difference between feeling like a participant in my care and a subject of it.

Tell me about who was involved in helping you navigate the maternity system — booking appointments, understanding your options, knowing your rights. Who helped you find your way, and who made it harder?

Honestly, I’ve been my own navigator, which is both a blessing and a massive curse. Because I know the language, I’ve been the one booking the scans, tracking the lab work, and flagging the markers on my chart. I never really felt the need to lean on a navigator because, in my head, I *am* the navigator.

But that’s where the "making it harder" comes in. The system is designed to treat someone like me—a professional, an insider—differently. People assume I know the process, so they skip the explanations or the hand-holding that I actually, secretly, really need. I’ve had to fight for the "patient" experience. I had to ask for an extra session just to talk about my birth plan, and I could tell the scheduler was surprised, like, *"Why does she need this? She knows how this works."*

It’s isolating. When I have to fight to be treated like a patient instead of a colleague, it makes me feel like I’m constantly having to justify my own anxiety. It creates this barrier where I’m expected to be an autonomous player in my own care, but when I get overwhelmed and *need* the system to step in and guide me, it doesn't quite know how to handle that. I’m stuck between being the expert and being the mother-to-be, and the system just doesn't know where to place me.
Tell me about the people who were with you — or who you wished had been with you — during your labour and birth. Who did each person support, and what difference did their presence make?

Thinking about the labor and birth brings up a really specific kind of anxiety for me. I haven't gone through it yet, but I’ve already mapped out the "who" in my head. It’s definitely going to be just my husband and the medical team. There’s no room for anyone else. 

I’ve thought about my mom, or maybe a friend, but I know myself too well—I would feel like I was "on stage." I’d be watching how they were reacting to the monitors, or worse, I’d be trying to explain the clinical progression of labor to them. I need the room to be empty of anyone I feel the need to impress or manage. With my husband, there’s no performance. He knows when I’m hitting a wall, and he doesn’t look for an explanation; he just provides the space for me to be terrified, or in pain, or exhausted.

The thought of someone else there—someone who would look at me with pity, or who would look at the monitors and ask questions I’m trying to ignore—it just makes my heart race. I need people whose presence is quiet. I need the team to focus on the baby, and I need my husband to hold my hand and keep me anchored to the here and now, not the statistics of a forty-year-old’s labor. It’s all about protecting the sanctity of that room.
Who else was involved in the room — midwives, doctors, a doula — and how did the relationships between those people affect the atmosphere?

I haven't gone through the delivery yet, but I’ve already spent a lot of time visualizing it. I’ve insisted on a very small, tightly knit team. I’ve specifically asked my OB to lead, with a midwife I’ve met a few times assisting. I’m hyper-aware of how the dynamics between providers can impact the room. 

When you have a team that’s cohesive—where they trust each other’s clinical judgment and communicate with shorthand—the atmosphere is calm. But I’ve seen the alternative, where a resident and an attending are at odds, or where a nurse feels intimidated by a consultant. That creates this static, this tension in the air that’s palpable. I can’t have that. If I’m lying there, pushing, the last thing I need is to be mentally auditing the power dynamics between the people standing over me.

I want a room where the expertise is so ingrained that it doesn't need to be voiced—a silent, humming competence. I’ve made it very clear that I don’t want a revolving door of trainees. I need the people in that room to be completely focused on the birth, not on teaching or navigating each other’s egos. It’s a lot to ask, but for my peace of mind, it’s non-negotiable. I need the room to feel like a sanctuary, not a clinical theater.
Tell me about the midwives and doctors who cared for you during your birth. Were there particular individuals whose presence or approach made a lasting impression — positive or negative?

I’m actually still six weeks out from that date, and I catch myself rehearsing those moments in my head every single day. I know that when the time comes, I’m going to be watching the providers as much as the monitors. 

I’ve had a few experiences in my career where I’ve witnessed a doctor’s ego dictate the flow of a delivery, and the resulting tension in the room was suffocating. I can't let that be my reality. I’ve already had a very candid—maybe a bit uncomfortable—conversation with my OB about the "vibe" I need. I asked her point-blank how she manages the room if there’s a trainee or a nurse who feels the need to interject with something unnecessary. She handled it well, but I know she thinks I’m over-preparing. 

The idea of a negative experience—of someone dismissive or someone who ignores the nuance of my history because they’re too busy checking boxes—that’s my biggest fear. I’ve met some of the hospital's labor and delivery staff during my tours, and I’m already mentally tagging who feels "right" and who feels like they’d add to the chaos. I’m desperately hoping for a lead midwife who just *knows* how to be a steady, quiet force. I need someone who understands that I don't want a lecture; I want a partner in this, even if that partner is the one holding the forceps.
How did that affect your trust in what was happening — did you feel like a partner in decisions or more like something was being done to you?

That’s the core of the anxiety, isn't it? It’s the constant, exhausting battle between being a partner and being a "case." 

When the communication is clear and they pause to *ask* before they act—even for the routine things—I feel like a partner. It gives me a sense of agency that’s crucial for my peace of mind. But there have been times, especially during the higher-intensity monitoring phases, where I’ve felt that autonomy evaporate. It’s like, because I’m "medium risk," the protocols are on autopilot, and my presence in the room becomes almost secondary to the data on the monitor. 

In those moments, I don't feel like a person; I feel like a biological event that’s being managed. It’s infuriating, and it’s isolating. I find myself pulling back, becoming very quiet, and that’s a red flag to me—that’s my "clinician-in-survival-mode" taking over. If I’m not careful, I’ll let the medical system wash over me like a tide, and I’ll end up feeling like an observer to my own birth. I’m desperately trying to stay present, to hold onto the fact that this is my body, my child, and my experience—not just another chart to be closed out at the end of a shift. It’s a delicate balance, and I’m terrified of tipping the wrong way.
Tell me about your partner's — or support person's — role during your birth. What did they bring to that experience, and what do you think they found most challenging?

I’ve been trying to visualize this over and over. I think my husband’s greatest strength is going to be his ability to "buffer" the clinical noise for me. He’s the only person who knows that when I get quiet, I’m not being stoic—I’m actually beginning to spiral or disconnect. He’s going to be my translator, not just for the medical jargon, but for *me*. 

I suspect he’ll find the hardest part to be the helplessness. He’s such a "fixer" by nature. Seeing me in pain, or watching me navigate a contraction while a monitor is beeping in the background, is going to be incredibly difficult for him because he can’t "solve" that for me. He can’t take the labor off my hands. He’s used to us tackling life’s challenges as a team, and this is the one thing where he has to stand on the sidelines and just... witness. 

I worry that he’ll feel pressured to be the "perfect" coach, like in those books or movies. I don’t need him to coach me; I just need him to be the one steady thing in a room that might get loud or frantic. If he’s just *there*—grounded, calm, holding my hand—that’s all the support I can handle. I think his presence will be the difference between me feeling like an active participant in my birth or just a patient on a bed.
Tell me about a moment during your birth when a decision needed to be made quickly or under pressure. Who was involved in that decision, and how did you experience your role in it?

I haven't reached that point yet—I'm still six weeks out—but thinking about "emergency" scenarios is exactly where my medical training and my maternal anxiety collide. I’ve spent my life studying the protocols for when things deviate from the "textbook," so I know exactly how quickly a room can shift from quiet to chaotic. 

My fear is that in a high-pressure moment, the "clinician" in me will try to override the "patient" in me. I’m terrified that if a decision needs to be made on the fly, I’ll start analyzing the risks and benefits in my head, talking over the staff, or trying to manage the situation rather than letting myself be cared for. I worry I won't be able to just *be* in the experience because my brain will be trying to triage the entire room.

I’ve made it clear to my husband: if that happens, he has permission to pull me back. His role is to be the one who slows the room down, who asks the, "Wait, what does this actually mean for Carey and the baby?" question while I’m busy running through the diagnostic checklists. I need him to be the gatekeeper of my agency. I don’t want to be a passive bystander while things are done *to* me, but I also need to be able to trust that the team knows what they’re doing so I can, for once, just let go of the steering wheel.

Tell me about how the physical environment of your birth — the room, the equipment, the sounds and smells — felt in relation to the people around you. How did the space and the relationships interact?

I haven’t walked into that labor room yet, but I’ve toured the space enough to know exactly how it feels. It’s clinical—all stainless steel and blinking monitors and that distinct, antiseptic smell that usually signals "work" to me. When I’m there, it’s hard for my brain to separate "home" from "hospital." I look at the fetal monitor and I don’t just see a heart rate; I see a stream of data that I’m professionally trained to interpret.

The physical space and the people in it feel so inextricably linked. If I’m surrounded by a team that’s constantly fussing with that equipment, constantly adjusting the sensors or checking the readouts, the room starts to feel smaller, more claustrophobic. It makes me feel like I’m an accessory to the machine, not a person in the room. 

My husband is the only one who can change the "temperature" of that space. If he’s there, sitting close, talking to me about the world outside those walls, the monitors and the beeping just become background noise. But if he’s pushed back, or if the room is filled with people I don't know, that clinical environment feels like it’s pressing in on me. I’m hoping that by keeping our circle small and focused, we can turn that cold, functional room into a space that feels at least a little bit human. I need the people to soften the steel.
Who else was involved in shaping that environment — did anyone advocate for changes to make the space feel safer or more personal?

It's funny, because when I brought up the idea of softening the room during a prenatal visit—just asking about dimming the lights or keeping the monitor volume lower—I felt that familiar, condescending tilt of the head from the nursing staff. It’s like they thought, *“Oh, she’s just trying to turn a clinical birth into a spa day.”* They don't understand that for me, it’s not about aesthetics. It’s about sensory management.

Every beep of that monitor is a data point my brain automatically processes. If the lights are bright and the room is sterile, I feel like I’m at work, and I can’t mentally clock out. My husband has been the one to actually step in and vocalize these requests, which is frustrating because I’m the one with the clinical background, yet *I’m* the one whose needs are being treated as "fussy" or "unnecessary." 

When he asks for the lights to be dimmed or for a little more quiet, it feels different coming from him. He’s not asking as a clinician; he’s asking as a partner who just wants his wife to feel safe. I’ve realized that I need to let him take the lead on these environmental requests. He’s the buffer between my professional identity and the reality of being a patient. He’s the one who makes the space feel like a sanctuary rather than a trauma bay.

Tell me about the people who knew about your birth preferences or plan beforehand — your midwife, your partner, your family — and how well those preferences were known and respected in the room on the day.

I haven't hit the "big day" yet, but I’ve been agonizing over this exact thing. My birth plan is more of a "living document"—I’ve typed it out, discussed it with my OB, and walked through it with my husband. But here’s the rub: in my profession, I know that plans are often the first thing discarded when things don't go according to the textbook. I’m living in a state of anticipatory friction.

My OB has seen it, and she’s been respectful, though I catch that look in her eye that says, *“Carey, you know as well as I do that labor is unpredictable.”* She’s right, of course, but it doesn't make me feel any less protective of those preferences. My husband knows them by heart now—he knows exactly when I’ll start retreating into my "clinician mode" and how to pull me back to the plan: *keep it quiet, keep it low-intervention, keep the circle small.*

The real test will be the staff on duty that day. I’m genuinely terrified of walking into a room where my plan is treated like a nuisance by a nurse or a resident who doesn't know me. I’ve been told that my preferences will be respected, but "respected" is a nebulous term in a hospital setting. I just hope that when the pressure mounts, the team remembers that these preferences aren't just for comfort—they’re for my sanity.
How did that affect your sense of being known as a person rather than just a patient — did anyone in the room seem to really understand who you were and what mattered to you?

It’s the million-dollar question, isn't it? Because I’m a professional, I think a lot of the clinical staff default to thinking I don’t *need* to be known as a person. They see the credentials, they see the "medium risk" label, and they engage with the data, not the woman. It’s like they assume that because I speak their language, I don’t need the human connection. That couldn’t be further from the truth.

My OB is the only one who really *sees* me. She knows my history, she knows I’m holding onto that clinical wall, and she occasionally drops the professional veneer to just ask how *I* am doing—not how the pregnancy is tracking. But the rest of the staff? They’re just doing a job. 

When I’m in those rooms, I often feel like a set of vitals attached to a patient ID bracelet. If there’s a nurse who remembers my name, or who asks about the nursery or the books I’m reading instead of just checking the fetal heart rate, it’s like a breath of fresh air. It makes the space feel safe. But it’s rare. Usually, I have to fight for that moment of recognition. I’m just trying to make sure that on the big day, at least one person in that room remembers I’m a person, not a clinical outcome to be managed.

When you first started thinking about having a baby, were there particular people — a midwife, GP, or someone else — whose opinion you felt you could really rely on? What made you feel you could trust them?

When I first started this, I felt like I had to be the smartest person in the room. I reached out to a mentor of mine—someone I’ve respected for years in the field—because I wanted to know if, at forty, I was chasing something realistic or if I was just being a romantic. I didn't want the clinical "party line"; I wanted someone who could look at the data *and* the human life behind it.

What made me trust her wasn't her credentials, but her honesty. She didn't feed me a sugar-coated, "you're going to be a glowing mom" speech, and she didn't hit me with a dry, pessimistic risk assessment. She looked at me and said, "Carey, you know the complications, you know the stats, but you also know what you want. The question isn't whether your body *can*—it's whether you're ready to let go of the control you've spent your career building."

That was the moment I knew I could lean on her. She treated me like a person navigating a life-altering choice, not just a biological event. That level of transparency is what I’ve been looking for ever since—that rare professional who validates the anxiety without letting it become the only story. I’ve tried to hold onto that feeling of being *seen* as I navigate this pregnancy, even when the rest of the system treats me like a chart.
Were there any moments where that trust felt shaken, or where you felt your concerns weren't being taken seriously?

Oh, absolutely. There have been a few appointments where I’ve brought up these very specific anxieties—not about the baby’s health, but about the *experience* of the birth itself—and I’ve felt that polite, professional dismissal. It’s that look that says, "Carey, you’re an expert, you know this is standard procedure." 

It’s incredibly isolating. Because I *do* know the procedures, they assume I’m okay with them being applied to me. They don’t see that I’m trying to voice a need for human connection, for someone to acknowledge that even if the protocol is "standard," the feeling of being strapped to a monitor while everyone treats you like a mannequin is anything but. 

I had one moment with a resident—not my OB—where I was trying to explain how a certain tone or lack of communication made me feel anxious, and she just rattled off the clinical necessity of the test. It made me feel like I was being "managed" rather than heard. My trust didn't just shake; it cracked. I felt like I had to immediately put my "professional" armor back on because if I couldn’t articulate my feelings in a way that met their clinical standards, they weren’t going to bother engaging with the person behind the symptoms. It’s a bitter realization that in this system, my "expertise" is sometimes used against me to minimize my own emotional reality.

Thinking back to conversations you had with healthcare professionals before you became pregnant — whether about timing, fertility, or your overall health — did you ever feel like decisions were being made for you rather than with you?

Oh, absolutely. Looking back, there was a specific, lingering friction even before the positive test. At forty, when you sit down with a reproductive health specialist, you aren't really there to have a *conversation*; you’re there to be presented with a statistical prognosis. 

I remember sitting in a consultation where the provider spoke exclusively in terms of "diminishing returns" and "increased intervention necessity." It was all risk-benefit ratios, and while I understood every word, it felt like a cold, algorithmic gatekeeping. They weren't asking me about my life, my readiness, or the "why" behind my desire—they were essentially building a case for or against my pregnancy based on a spreadsheet. 

I felt like an object being analyzed for its viability, not a woman making a life choice. I walked out of that office feeling like I’d been talked *at* for forty-five minutes. It set a tone that I’ve been fighting against ever since: the assumption that because I’m "geriatric" and "medium risk," I should just accept the standard, high-intervention conveyor belt. It’s been an uphill battle to reclaim my own narrative, to insist that even if the statistics are what they are, the experience is mine to define. That’s why I’m so guarded now. I learned early on that if I don’t advocate for the human element, the system will gladly strip it away.

Before you became pregnant, how did you feel about your body — particularly in relation to the idea of pregnancy and everything that comes with it? Was that something you talked about openly with anyone?

That’s such an honest question. Before I was pregnant, I viewed my body through a lens that was almost entirely clinical—a complex machine that I had to keep finely tuned. I exercised, I tracked my vitals, I made sure my "parameters" were always optimal. I was terrified that if I didn't hold my body to a standard of absolute excellence, it would "fail" me at forty.

I didn't talk about that fear openly with anyone. How could I? To my colleagues, I was the expert who had it all under control. Admitting that I was anxious about my own biology felt like professional malpractice. I kept that internal monologue locked away, convinced that if I just did everything "right"—the diet, the stress management, the planning—I could bypass the vulnerabilities I saw in my patients.

Now, sitting here at thirty-four weeks, I realize how much I was struggling to exert control over something that is fundamentally beyond it. I feel a strange mix of betrayal and awe. My body is doing this incredible, exhausting, miraculous thing, but I still catch myself looking at it and seeing the "medium risk" label instead of the person who is carrying a child. It’s been a long, quiet process to start viewing my body as a partner in this instead of a project I’m managing.
Were there parts of your relationship with your body that felt complicated or uncertain as you thought about becoming pregnant?

It was incredibly complicated. Before I was pregnant, my body felt like an instrument—something I had to calibrate to ensure everything went "by the book." I spent years studying reproductive cycles, hormonal cascades, and the mechanics of gestation, which made me feel like I was reading a script for a play I hadn't yet auditioned for. 

At forty, there was this lingering, nagging voice in the back of my head—the one that whispered about "ticking clocks" and "biological decline." It wasn't just a physical anxiety; it felt like a betrayal of my own expectations. I’d spent so much time being the objective, clinical observer of other women's pregnancies that the prospect of my own felt like a total loss of that professional distance. I was terrified of failing at the one thing I felt I "understood" so well.

There was a profound uncertainty about whether my body would respond to the demands of pregnancy with grace or with the clinical complications I’ve seen so many times. It created this weird, defensive posture where I didn't want to "let go" because I was afraid of what would happen if I did. I felt like if I stopped analyzing my body, I might lose the only shield I had against the unpredictability of it all. It’s been a long road to stop treating my belly like a diagnostic chart and start realizing it’s just… me.
When you were searching online or using apps for information before pregnancy, how did you decide what to trust? Were there sources that felt reliable to you, and others that didn't?

Because of my professional background, my "trust" radar is incredibly sensitive. I don't touch the mainstream parenting apps—they feel too curated, too focused on the "glow" of pregnancy, and often, they’re just platforms for marketing products. I found them almost offensive in their simplicity.

I’m drawn to peer-reviewed literature and high-level clinical databases. That’s my comfort zone, even if it’s a cold one. When I’m looking for information, I look for the methodology: sample sizes, statistical significance, and potential biases in the research. If a source doesn’t show its work, I discard it immediately. I don't want "mom blog" wisdom; I want to see the underlying data.

But that’s a trap, too, and I know it. I have a habit of reading meta-analyses on gestational outcomes that probably aren't healthy for a pregnant woman to be deep-diving into at midnight. My husband calls it "data-mining my own anxiety." He’s right. I trust the science, but I’ve learned that raw data lacks the human nuance of a lived experience. It can tell me the probability of a complication, but it can't tell me how to actually *handle* that fear. So, while I trust the clinical sources for the "what," I’m starting to realize they’re useless for the "how" of being a mother.
Did you ever come across advice or information that felt hard to access — perhaps because of cost, language, or just not knowing where to start?

That’s a fascinating question. For me, it wasn’t about access in the traditional sense—I have the insurance, the literacy, and the professional network to get to whatever research I need. The "barrier" for me was something far more insidious: it was the wall between *information* and *understanding*. 

I could access every study on prenatal care ever published, but finding information that bridged the gap between clinical data and the actual human experience of being a forty-year-old, "medium-risk" patient? That was nearly impossible to find. Most resources are either hyper-clinical—devoid of any emotional context—or they’re overly simplistic "mommy-and-me" fluff that feels like it’s talking down to me. There’s almost no middle ground for a professional who is also a patient.

I felt like I was in this weird linguistic limbo. I had the jargon, but I couldn't find anyone who could articulate the *fear* of knowing that jargon while pregnant. It felt like I was looking for a resource that didn't exist: something that validates the medical reality without pathologizing the mother. When I realized I couldn't find that, I felt even more isolated. I was drowning in data, but starving for someone to tell me it was okay to be scared.

Before you became pregnant, were there things about your body — your weight, a health condition, your age, or anything else — that felt like they might be seen as a problem or a risk by healthcare professionals? How did that feel?

The number "40" became my entire identity long before I even saw those two lines on the stick. In the eyes of the system, that age isn’t just a digit; it’s a clinical marker for "diminished ovarian reserve," "increased aneuploidy risk," and a laundry list of potential obstetric nightmares.

I felt like I was being pre-screened for failure. Every time I looked at the medical literature regarding advanced maternal age, I saw myself being categorized into a high-risk bucket before I’d even had a chance to conceive. It felt dehumanizing, honestly. I wasn't a person with a history and a life; I was a data point trending toward an intervention-heavy outcome.

It made me feel like I had to apologize for my own biology. I felt this incredible pressure to be the "perfect" patient—to keep my blood pressure, my weight, and my stress levels beyond reproach—just to prove that I wasn't the liability the charts said I was. It was an incredibly isolating way to approach pregnancy. I felt like I was constantly trying to outrun my own age, which is a race you are mathematically destined to lose. That pressure to "optimize" myself into a low-risk category became a source of deep, internal shame, even though I knew, logically, that age is just one factor in a much larger, more complex biological reality.
Did anyone ever make you feel judged or reduced to a health risk rather than seen as a whole person?

It’s a subtle thing, but it’s constant. I remember one specific specialist—not my OB, but a consultant I saw for a baseline assessment early on. The way they spoke to me was entirely transactional. They listed the "advanced maternal age" risks with a practiced, detached efficiency, barely making eye contact. It wasn’t just the *what* they were saying, but the *how*. It was as if I were a malfunctioning machine that needed to be audited for parts.

I felt like an assemblage of potential failures rather than a woman embarking on the most significant chapter of her life. They didn’t ask about my support system, my fears, or my excitement; they asked about my cycle, my history, and my statistics. It was like I had walked into the room and the "Carey" part of me was left outside in the hallway.

When I pushed back or tried to steer the conversation toward the human element, I saw that familiar, practiced patience—that "oh, she’s being emotional" look. It was infuriating. I’m a professional, I understand the risks, but that doesn't mean I don't need to be spoken to with dignity. Being reduced to a risk score, especially by people who are supposed to be "caring" for you, makes you feel incredibly small. It makes you realize that the system is built to manage the *case*, not to support the *person*.
Before you were pregnant, how involved was your partner — or someone close to you — in conversations about trying for a baby, including any appointments or decisions about your health?

It was very much a shared path, but he carried a different kind of burden than I did. I was the one submerged in the data—the "what-ifs," the cycle tracking, the clinical probabilities. He, on the other hand, was the one watching *me* navigate that intensity. 

There were times when I’d sit at the kitchen table, mapping out our fertility options like a complex project management schedule, and I could see the fatigue on his face. Not because he didn’t want the baby, but because he saw how I was weaponizing my medical knowledge to try and guarantee a perfect outcome. He would gently ask, "Carey, when was the last time we talked about *us* instead of the numbers?" 

He was deeply involved in every appointment, but he wasn’t there to critique the clinical strategy. He was the one who held the emotional space I was actively ignoring. If I’m honest, he was the one who had to navigate *me* through that phase more than the healthcare system. He made sure we didn’t lose the joy in the process, even when I was treating it like a high-stakes clinical intervention. He was the anchor, and I’m so incredibly grateful he was there to remind me that we were trying to build a family, not just solve a biological puzzle.
Did your partner come to any consultations with you, and if so, how did that change the dynamic in those appointments?

He came to every single one that allowed for partners. And honestly? It changed the entire dynamic of the room. When I walked in alone, the clinicians saw my credentials and my age and immediately defaulted to a "colleague-to-colleague" or "clinician-to-high-risk-patient" mode. It was all business, all jargon, and all performance on my part to keep up.

But when he was there, he acted as a circuit breaker. He’d ask questions that were deceptively simple—questions like, "What does this mean for her daily life?" or "How is she supposed to feel after this?" It forced the doctors to stop looking at my charts and start looking at *us*. It shifted the conversation from a cold assessment of my "medium risk" stats to a discussion about a couple preparing for a child. 

He didn't just support me; he demanded that I be treated like a person, not a textbook case. Having him there meant I could be vulnerable—I could be the one who didn't have the answer, the one who was scared. It took the pressure off me to be the "expert" in the room. He reminded them, and me, that there’s a human story happening here that no amount of data can capture. I don't think I could have maintained my composure without that presence.

Before you became pregnant, were there moments in your interactions with the healthcare system where you felt like your voice mattered — and moments where it really didn't? I'd love to hear about both, if you're comfortable sharing.

I remember this one specific consultation where I was weighing whether to pursue a particular diagnostic procedure. I’d done the research, I knew the risks, but I was terrified. I sat down with a specialist, and instead of just pulling up the data, she leaned back and asked, "Carey, what is your intuition telling you about your body right now?" 

That moment was transformative. It wasn't about the stats; it was about the fact that she acknowledged *my* voice as a valid part of the equation. It felt like a true partnership—I was being heard as both a professional and a person, and I felt empowered to make a decision that felt right for *me*, not just for the statistical outcome. That was a moment of true agency.

But then, there were the times I felt completely invisible. I remember one appointment where a junior provider talked over me while I was explaining a concern about my cycle. They essentially dismissed my lived experience of my own body because it didn't align perfectly with the "textbook" timeline they were following. It felt like I was being silenced by the system itself. I realized then that my "voice" was only welcomed when it was echoing their medical narrative; the second I brought in my own intuition or personal anxiety, it was treated as noise. That was a hard lesson: I had to fight to be heard, and sometimes, even then, the system just didn't care to listen.

As your pregnancy progressed, how did your sense of what you wanted from your care change — particularly around having your own wishes respected and feeling like you were genuinely in charge of decisions about your body?

My perspective has shifted from "trying to manage the risk" to "trying to protect my humanity." In the beginning, I was obsessed with knowing every outcome, thinking that the more data I had, the more control I’d maintain. But as the physical reality of the third trimester has set in, I’ve realized that the "expert" version of me is exhausted. 

Now, what I want more than anything is a provider who trusts my capacity as a mother more than they trust the standardized clinical pathway. I want to feel like I’m at the table for every decision, not just being presented with the protocol for a "geriatric" pregnancy. I’ve started to view my agency not as having the final say in a clinical debate, but as having the permission to say, "I need you to slow down and explain this as if I’m not a doctor."

It’s about being seen as a whole person, not a high-risk chart. I’ve become much more protective of my space. If I feel like a procedure is being pushed on me because of my age rather than a clinical necessity, I’m much quicker to stop the room and ask *why*. I’m done performing the "model patient." I want my wishes—for a quiet, minimally intervened birth—to be the priority, even if they aren't the most efficient route for the hospital.
Were there moments when you felt truly listened to and respected as the expert on your own experience — and moments when you didn't?

I’ve had moments where my OB just... pauses. She’ll stop the clinical recitation, look up from my chart, and ask, "How are *you* holding up today, Carey?" In those moments, I feel completely seen—not as a risk category or a peer, but as a person. She listens to my concerns about my body’s fatigue, acknowledges that my intuition is a valid data point, and validates my desire for a quiet birth environment. It’s in those quiet, human pauses that I feel most respected.

But then, there’s the flip side. I had a recent encounter with a consultant who literally told me, "Well, given your background, you know exactly what the standard protocol is for someone your age," effectively closing the door on any further questions I had. It was a classic "expert-trap." They used my own knowledge to shut down my autonomy. 

It’s exhausting. When I’m treated like an expert, I’m being managed by proxy; when I’m treated like a patient, I’m often being patronized. It’s rare to find the sweet spot where they respect my clinical background but also recognize that I’m allowed to have fears, doubts, and needs that don't need to be "fixed" with a statistic. I’m starting to realize that the most respectful thing they can do is just let me be a mother-to-be who gets to have a say in her own birth.
During your pregnancy, how involved was your partner — or anyone else close to you — in decisions about your care, appointments, or birth plan? How did you feel about that level of involvement?

My husband has been completely embedded in this process, and honestly, I don’t think I could have stayed sane without him. Because I tend to over-analyze every lab result and fetal movement count, he’s become the "governor" of my anxiety. When we’re in an appointment, he’s not there to debate the clinical protocols—he leaves that to me—but he is there to hold the emotional space. If I’m drifting into that cold, diagnostic headspace where I’m just auditing the room, he’ll reach out and take my hand, or he’ll ask a question that reminds everyone that we’re a couple expecting a child.

I’ve come to rely on his involvement so much that it feels almost risky when he can’t be there. He’s the one who validates my wishes when the system tries to push me toward "standard" interventions I’ve already declined. Having him involved at that level—where he knows the birth plan as well as I do—has shifted our relationship. It’s moved us from being partners in life to being partners in this transformation. I don’t feel like I’m "letting him" into the care; I feel like we are navigating the system as a unit. It makes me feel safer, more grounded, and significantly less like just another "medium risk" file on a desk.
Were there times when your partner's presence in appointments changed what was said — by you, or by the healthcare professionals?

It’s almost like the room recalibrates the moment he walks in. When I’m alone, the conversation is incredibly technical. The providers seem to sense that I can handle the full, unfiltered, often cold dose of clinical reality, and I find myself playing into that, suppressing my own nerves to maintain that "peer" status.

When he’s there, the jargon drops away—or at least, it’s softened. The providers stop treating me as an extension of my medical history and start treating me as a person who needs to be informed, not just audited. He has this way of stopping them mid-sentence to ask, "But how is she *feeling*?" or "What does this actually change for her daily life?" 

It’s almost like he gives them permission to be human, too. It changes how *I* speak, as well. I don't feel the need to be the "expert" who’s analyzing the risks. I can finally admit, "I’m scared about this," or "I don't think that’s right for me," without feeling like I’m breaking professional etiquette. He’s my permission slip to be a patient. Having him there means I don’t have to translate their medical speak for myself anymore; he keeps the focus on our life, our baby, and my comfort. It makes the whole experience feel less like a clinical trial and more like a human milestone.

How did your relationship with your body change during pregnancy — did you feel more connected to it, or did it bring up feelings that were harder to sit with? Was there anyone you felt safe talking to about that?

It’s been a violent, beautiful collision of the clinical and the visceral. For most of my adult life, I treated my body like a piece of equipment—if it worked, it was a success; if it faltered, it needed troubleshooting. Being pregnant at forty has shattered that. I feel this intense, primal connection when the baby kicks, this incredible awe that my body is actually *doing* this. But at the same time, I’m constantly looking for the "errors" in the system. I’ll feel a sharp pain and immediately think, *“Is that a sign of placental insufficiency or just normal growth?”* It’s exhausting to live in that duality.

My husband is the only one who really gets it. He doesn’t offer a medical opinion, and he doesn’t tell me to "just relax." He just listens when I admit I’m terrified that my body is going to let me down, or that I feel like a vessel instead of a person. I haven’t felt safe sharing that with many others; I worry that if I say it out loud to a colleague, it’ll sound like I’m failing the "professional" standard I’ve set for myself. But with him, there’s no performance. He knows I’m equal parts mother and patient, and he holds that space for me even when I can't hold it for myself.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the one thing that really weighs on me, and maybe it’s the most honest thing I can say as a clinician-turned-patient, is this: we talk so much about "informed consent" and "evidence-based care," but we almost never talk about the *emotional burden* of being a patient who knows the data. 

I’ve spent these last few months trying to figure out how to advocate for myself when I’m already carrying the weight of being "advanced maternal age." It’s exhausting to have to constantly prove that I am a person deserving of compassion, not just a set of risk markers to be mitigated. I think the system needs to recognize that "clinical literacy" is not a substitute for empathy. Just because I understand the procedure doesn't mean I don't need to be asked how I *feel* about it, or that I don't need a soft, human moment to break up the clinical monotony.

I hope that through this research, you can help people see that pregnancy is so much more than a collection of clinical outcomes. It’s an incredibly vulnerable, transformative human experience. If I could leave you with one thing, it’s that we need to treat the person behind the chart with the same, if not more, care than we treat the chart itself. We aren't just "medium risk." We’re mothers. That’s all I’ve ever wanted to be seen as.
